metadata.summary: RAG evaluation strategy: metric selection by use case and stage, cost-tiered execution, sampling, quality gates, and common evaluation pitfalls.

content.title: RAG Evaluation Strategy
content.summary: RAG evaluation strategy defines which metrics to run, when to run them, how to sample to control LLM-judge costs, and how to set explicit numeric quality gates. The core principle: retrieval metrics (free, run always) + generation metrics (expensive, run on 10-20% sample) + explicit numeric thresholds before deployment.

why.p: Without a deliberate evaluation strategy, teams default to one familiar metric and use "looks good" as a quality gate. This leads to blind-spot regressions: a model that improves faithfulness while degrading retrieval recall, or a retriever that maintains Precision@5 while Hit Rate slowly decays on a new document category. Cost is also a practical constraint — running GPT-4o faithfulness evaluation on every query is orders of magnitude more expensive than sampling. A strategy specifies what to measure, at what cost tier, and what numeric threshold constitutes a pass.

list.item: Before adding any RAG evaluation — use this methodology to decide which metrics to implement first.
Before deploying a new retriever or generator — derive deployment quality gate thresholds from this strategy.
When LLM-judge evaluation costs are becoming a concern — apply cost-tier sampling rules here.
When a metric is trending downward in production — use evaluation stage guidance to escalate from Hit Rate (cheap) to full RAGAS suite.
After the pipeline is already built with hardcoded metrics — at that point read rag-eval-retrieval-metrics and rag-eval-generation-metrics for the specific implementations.
When evaluating a single query interactively — strategy is for systematic batch evaluation, not one-off debugging.

section.name: metric-selection-by-stage
section.p: Different evaluation stages justify different metric breadth. Expand coverage as the pipeline approaches production:

list.item: Development: Precision@5, Recall@5, Faithfulness — quick feedback on retrieval and hallucination, minimal cost
Pre-production: Full RAGAS suite (faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall) + NDCG@K — comprehensive before deployment
Production monitoring: Hit Rate, Faithfulness (sampled 10-20%), Latency — cheap continuous signal; escalate on regression
A/B testing: End-to-end accuracy, User satisfaction (human eval or implicit signals) — measures what users actually care about

section.name: metric-selection-by-use-case
section.p: Select the primary metric bundle based on the RAG use case:

list.item: Question Answering: Faithfulness + Answer Relevance + Precision@3 — small K because Q&A benefits from precision over breadth
Document Search: NDCG@10 + Recall@20 + MRR — large K, breadth matters, graded relevance
Chatbot: Context Relevance + Coherence + User satisfaction — conversational quality beyond factual accuracy
Summarization: Faithfulness + Completeness + Conciseness — faithfulness is critical; completeness and conciseness are opposing tensions

section.name: cost-tiers
section.p: Metric cost tiers — select by budget and evaluation stage:

list.item: Free tier: Precision, Recall, MRR, NDCG, Hit Rate — no API cost; run on every test batch; use numpy only
Low cost: RAGAS with small model (e.g., gpt-4o-mini) — regular testing; approximately $0.01-0.10 per 100 queries
Medium cost: LLM-based metrics with gpt-4o-mini for faithfulness — important evaluations; ~3x cheaper than gpt-4o with ~5% accuracy loss
High cost: Full suite with gpt-4o — critical pre-deployment decisions; reserve for final quality gate

rule.statement: LLM-judge metrics (faithfulness, answer relevance, context relevance) MUST run on a sampled subset (10-20%) of production queries, not on every query. Retrieval metrics (precision, recall, MRR, hit rate, NDCG) MUST run on the full labeled test set.
rule.rationale: LLM-judge calls cost money and add latency. Running faithfulness on 15% of queries captures statistically meaningful trends while keeping eval costs 5-7x lower. Retrieval metrics have zero API cost and should cover the full test set to avoid sampling noise in rank-quality measurements.

section.name: sampling-strategy
section.p: Sampling strategies for LLM-judge evaluation batches:

example.title: Smart sampling for cost-controlled evaluation
example.code:
import random from typing import List, Dict
def smart_sampling( test_set: List[dict], budget: int, strategy: str = "diverse" ) -> List[dict]: """ Sample test cases based on budget and strategy.
Strategies: - diverse: Cover different question types evenly - difficult: Focus on cases where model previously failed - random: Random sampling (baseline)
Args: test_set: List of test case dicts with 'type', 'score_history' fields budget: Maximum number of cases to evaluate strategy: Sampling strategy """ if strategy == "diverse": # Group by question type, sample evenly across groups groups: Dict[str, List[dict]] = {} for case in test_set: q_type = case.get("type", "unknown") groups.setdefault(q_type, []).append(case) sampled = [] per_group = max(1, budget // len(groups)) for group_cases in groups.values(): sampled.extend(random.sample(group_cases, min(per_group, len(group_cases)))) return sampled[:budget] elif strategy == "difficult": # Prioritize cases where model previously scored below threshold difficult = [c for c in test_set if c.get("last_faithfulness", 1.0) < 0.80] easy = [c for c in test_set if c.get("last_faithfulness", 1.0) >= 0.80] sampled = difficult[:budget] remaining = budget - len(sampled) if remaining > 0: sampled.extend(random.sample(easy, min(remaining, len(easy)))) return sampled else: # Random sampling return random.sample(test_set, min(budget, len(test_set)))

rule.statement: Quality gates MUST be numeric and explicit before any production deployment: for example "faithfulness >= 0.80 AND precision@5 >= 0.70 AND hit_rate >= 0.90". "Looks good" or "metrics are acceptable" are not quality gates.
rule.rationale: Vague quality gates cannot be enforced in CI and create reviewer subjectivity. Numeric gates are runnable as assertions, blockable in deployment pipelines, and comparable across time. Track metric trends over 2+ weeks — a metric trending downward is actionable even if the current value is above threshold.

rule.statement: Quality gates MUST include at least one retrieval metric AND at least one generation metric. Never gate on a single metric.
rule.rationale: A model can improve faithfulness while degrading retrieval recall. A single-metric gate misses this cross-dimension regression. Minimum viable gate: faithfulness (generation) + precision@5 (retrieval).

example.title: CI quality gate assertion
example.code: def check_quality_gates(metrics: dict) -> list: """ Returns list of failed gates. Empty list = all pass. Raise or block deployment if any gate fails. """ gates = [ ("faithfulness", 0.80), ("precision@5", 0.70), ("hit_rate", 0.90), ] failures = [] for metric_name, threshold in gates: value = metrics.get(metric_name) if value is None: failures.append(f"{metric_name}: not computed") elif value < threshold: failures.append(f"{metric_name}={value:.3f} < {threshold}") return failures

antipattern.description: Single Metric Trap — relying on one metric (e.g., only faithfulness or only Hit Rate) as the sole quality signal.
antipattern.reason: No single metric fully characterizes RAG quality. Faithfulness can be high while the retriever returns irrelevant context. Hit Rate can be 1.0 while Precision@5 is 0.20. Always use a combination covering both retrieval and generation dimensions.

antipattern.description: No Baseline — measuring metric values without a stored baseline to compare against.
antipattern.reason: An absolute score of 0.75 is meaningless without knowing whether that is better or worse than the previous version. Store baseline metric values at every release and compare explicitly. A 5-point regression is actionable even if 0.75 seems "acceptable".

antipattern.description: Non-representative Test Set — using a convenience sample that doesn't reflect production query distribution.
antipattern.reason: Metrics computed on easy or in-distribution queries overestimate real-world quality. Include difficult queries (ambiguous, multi-hop, out-of-domain) and edge cases in the test set. Update the test set as production queries evolve.

antipattern.description: Static Ground Truth — never updating the ground-truth relevant doc IDs and expected answers as the document corpus evolves.
antipattern.reason: Ground truth becomes stale as new documents are added, old ones removed, or expected answer wording changes. A retrieval metric computed against outdated ground truth is measuring the wrong thing. Schedule ground-truth reviews with corpus changes.

antipattern.description: Evaluator Bias — treating LLM-judge scores as ground truth without acknowledging the evaluator's own biases and inconsistencies.
antipattern.reason: LLM evaluators have systematic biases: preference for verbosity, self-preference (evaluating own outputs higher), and position bias (scoring first item in a list higher). Calibrate LLM-judge scores against human labels on a sample before relying on them as quality gates.

list.item: Evaluation agent selects metric set based on use case: Q&A → faithfulness + precision@5 + MRR; document search → NDCG@10 + recall@20; chatbot → context relevance + hit rate.
Runs retrieval metrics (free tier) on full labeled test set.
Samples 10-20% of queries for LLM-based generation metrics to control cost.
Aggregates results and compares against stored baseline values.
Regression detection: flags any metric that dropped more than 5 percentage points vs. baseline.
Returns structured JSON: metrics dict, flags list, recommendation string.

example.title: Agent prompt pattern for RAG metrics evaluation

checklist.check: Metric bundle selected for the specific use case (Q&A / search / chatbot / summarization).
Evaluation stage matched to metric depth (dev / pre-prod / prod-monitoring / A/B).
Retrieval metrics (free tier) scheduled to run on every test batch.
LLM-judge metrics running on 10-20% sample, not every query.
Quality gates are numeric: at minimum faithfulness threshold + one retrieval metric threshold.
Baseline metric values stored at current release for regression comparison.
Test set reviewed for representativeness; includes edge cases and difficult queries.
Ground truth scheduled for review when document corpus changes.
